sanskrit_devanagari:
बुद्धियुक्तो जहातीह उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते ।
तस्माद्योगाय युज्यस्व योगः कर्मसु कौशलम् ॥ ५० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte |
tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam || 50 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; single danda terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.50।।)
IAST exact (buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte / tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.050 = MBh_06,024.050). Body text identical: 'buddhiyukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛtaduṣkṛte / tasmād yogāya yujyasva yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam'. GRETIL writes the compounds without hyphens; semantic content identical.

anvaya.meaning: joined / united (in compound buddhi-yuktaḥ: 'one joined to buddhi')
and bad actions / demerit (in compound sukṛta-duṣkṛte: 'good and bad actions')
to yoga (dative; for the sake of yoga)

translations.verbatim_quote: A man engaged in devotional service rids himself of both good and bad reactions even in this life. Therefore strive for yoga, which is the art of all work.

One who prudently practices the science of work without attachment can get rid of both good and bad reactions in this life itself. Therefore, strive for Yog, which is the art of working skillfully (in proper consciousness).

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Since time immemorial each living entity has accumulated the various reactions of his good and bad work. As such, he is continuously ignorant of his real constitutional position. One's ignorance can be removed by the instruction of the Bhagavad-gītā, which teaches one to surrender unto Lord Śrī Kṛṣṇa in all respects.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.50 Buddhi-yuktah, possessed of wisdom, possessed of the wisdom of equanimity; since one jahati, rejects; iha, here, in this world; ubhe, both; sukrta-duskrte, virtue and vice (righteousness and unrighteousness), through the purification of the mind and acquisition of Knowledge; tasmat, therefore; yujyasva, devote yourself; yogaya, to (Karma-) yoga, the wisdom of equanimity. For Yoga is kausalam, skilfulness; karmasu, in action. Skilfulness means the attitude of the skilful, the wisdom of equanimity with regard to one's success and failure while engaged in actions.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads kauśalam as 'skilfulness' explicitly and defines it: 'the attitude of the skilful, the wisdom of equanimity with regard to one's success and failure while engaged in actions.' Yoga = the skilful attitude that converts ordinary action (which would normally bind through sukṛta/duṣkṛte) into action that does not bind. The buddhi-yukta — one joined to discriminating intelligence — sheds both kinds of accumulation HERE (iha), in this very life, through purification of mind and acquisition of Knowledge. The verse is therefore a direct reason-clause for the practice of yoga: yoga is the skill that lets action issue without binding the actor. CRITICAL for engineering layer: kauśalam is SKILL OF ACTING (samatva applied during action), not effort, passion, or presence.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.50 He, who is established in evenness of mind in the performance of actions, relinquishes good and evil Karmas which have accumulated from time immemorial causing bondage endlessly. Therefore acquire this aforesaid evenness of mind (Buddhi Yoga). Yoga is skill in action. That is, this evenness of mind when one is engaged in action, is possible through great skill, i.e., ability.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads kauśalam as 'skill / ability' — the verse's claim is that maintaining evenness of mind WHILE engaged in action is itself possible only through great skill (mahā-kauśala). Yoga is named as that skill. The buddhi-yukta sheds beginningless accumulation of good and bad karma — those that bind the soul through saṁsāra — and the verse closes with the concise definition: yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam. The doctrinal point Ramanuja foregrounds: this is skill OF ACTION, not skill REPLACING ACTION. The verse rejects renunciation-of-action and rejects unskilful-action; what is praised is skilful-action, where skill = evenness held during the act.

positions.rendering: the art of working skillfully (in proper consciousness)
skilfulness — the attitude of the skilful, the wisdom of equanimity with regard to success and failure while engaged in actions
skill in action — i.e. evenness of mind during action, possible only through great ability

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: kauśalam is the verse's terminological pivot. Lexically it means 'skill, expertise, craft, dexterity' (from kuśala, 'skilled, fit, well'). Both Shankara and Ramanuja anchor it tightly: skill IS evenness-during-action; not generic effort, not passion, not enthusiasm, not 'doing things mindfully' in a vague sense. The skill named is specifically the kauśalam of holding samatvam (evenness) while performing the act. Modern translations 'art of work' / 'art of working skillfully' preserve this if one reads them strictly as skill-of-acting; the looser readings ('passion-driven action', 'skillful presence', 'flow') drift away from the classical commentary. Engineering layers must preserve kauśalam as SKILL OF ACTING (the senior who does in 30 minutes what others do in two days because they have internalised the principles), not as effort or enthusiasm.

positions.rendering: virtue and vice (righteousness and unrighteousness) — both are shed through buddhi-yoga
good and evil Karmas accumulated from time immemorial, causing endless bondage

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four traditions agree that BOTH sukṛta and duṣkṛte are shed by the buddhi-yukta — not just the bad. This is doctrinally important and counter-intuitive: even good karma binds, because both kinds chain the actor to the result-system. Shankara reads this through the Advaita lens (purification of mind + acquisition of Knowledge releases both); Ramanuja reads it through Vishishtadvaita (devotional renunciation of fruit-attachment releases the soul from bondage). The engineering layer must preserve the surprising claim: shipping for praise binds the senior just as much as shipping for fear-of-blame does. Both are forms of phala-attachment.

literal_meaning: One joined to buddhi sheds, here in this life, both good and bad action. Therefore engage yourself in yoga; yoga is skillfulness in action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 2.50 is a definitional verse with a famous closing pāda: yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam — 'yoga is skillfulness in action.' Three doctrinal moves. First: the buddhi-yukta — one joined to discriminating intelligence — sheds both sukṛta (merit) and duṣkṛte (demerit) here (iha), in this very life. The releasing power is buddhi-yoga, not action itself. Second: therefore (tasmāt) engage in yoga (yogāya yujyasva). Third: yoga is named definitionally as kauśalam — skill in action. Both Shankara and Ramanuja anchor kauśalam tightly: the skill named is specifically the skill of holding samatvam (evenness) while performing actions. The verse does NOT say yoga is 'effort', 'passion', 'flow', or 'mindful presence' in the loose modern senses — it says yoga is the SKILL of acting from the settled state. This is one of the most-quoted Gita verses and the one most easily flattened by careless modern translation.

cross_references.explanation: 2.39 introduced karma-yoga as a separate discipline from sāṅkhya. 2.50 gives karma-yoga its definitional name: yoga is kauśalam in action.

2.48 named yoga as samatvam (evenness in success and failure). 2.50 names yoga as kauśalam in action. The two definitions are complementary: skill in action IS the practical expression of evenness, and evenness IS the inner reality of skilful action.

2.49 ranked buddhi-yoga above fruit-driven action and called the latter pitiable. 2.50 explains why: buddhi-yoga sheds the binding-effect of action; fruit-driven action accumulates it. The ranking and the mechanism connect.

BG 4.18 — 'one who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is intelligent among humans, a yogin' — extends the kauśalam idea: skill in action includes seeing through the apparent action-inaction dichotomy. The pivot in 2.50 is foundational; chapter 4 elaborates.

remaining_gaps: Abhinavagupta's Kashmir Shaiva (Trika) commentary on this verse not pulled — would offer a different angle on kauśalam.
